question: Explain the molecular mechanism by which tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy mediates tumor regression in metastatic gastrointestinal cancers and contrast this with checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy.
answer:
Tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy operates through a fundamentally different mechanistic paradigm compared with checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy. TIL therapy involves the ex vivo expansion and reinfusion of autologous tumor-reactive T cells derived from the patient’s own tumor microenvironment. The molecular basis of TIL efficacy lies in their ability to recognize heterogeneous tumor antigens, including neoantigens, presented on major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I molecules. These T cells often express high-affinity TCRs that have evaded central tolerance and have been conditioned by prior tumor exposure to express cytotoxic molecules such as granzyme B and perforin, enabling direct tumor cell lysis. Unlike checkpoint inhibitors, which rely on systemic modulation of immune checkpoints such as PD-1/PD-L1 or CTLA-4 to enhance endogenous T cell responses, TIL therapy provides a pre-selected, numerically expanded, and tumor-specific T cell population capable of immediate antitumor activity. In metastatic gastrointestinal cancers, TILs often demonstrate oligoclonal TCR repertoires targeting shared antigens such as NY-ESO-1, MAGE-A family proteins, and KRAS driver mutations, particularly in tumors with high mutational burden. A recent phase II trial (NCT05897905) demonstrated a 42% objective response rate in metastatic colorectal cancer patients refractory to checkpoint blockade, with durable responses linked to the clonal expansion of T cells bearing TCRs specific for KRAS G12D and G12V mutations. In contrast, checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab in MSI-H colorectal cancer achieve approximately 40% response rates but often require months to induce clinical benefit and rely on intact host T cell function. Thus, TIL therapy complements checkpoint inhibitors by providing a “ready-to-attack” cellular product that bypasses tumor-induced immune exhaustion. While checkpoint inhibitors act as immunological “brake-release,” TIL therapy represents a “cell-based transplant” strategy that directly delivers cytotoxic T cells into the tumor bed. Emerging data suggest synergy when TILs are combined with lymphodepletion preconditioning regimens (e.g., cyclophosphamide/fludarabine) to deplete regulatory T cells and enhance TIL engraftment. These findings underscore the complementary roles of cellular and checkpoint immunotherapies in solid tumors.

Summary: TIL therapy achieves tumor regression by delivering expanded, tumor-specific cytotoxic T cells with high-affinity TCRs, directly lysing tumor cells via granzyme/perforin, independently of host immune state. Unlike checkpoint inhibitors, which modulate endogenous T cell responses over time, TIL therapy provides immediate antitumor activity and shows clinical synergy when combined with lymphodepletion. Integrating these modalities may optimize outcomes in gastrointestinal malignancies.
